So combined science and lifting weights and kind of ended up here and the Russians decided to kidnap you for a year and all of that somewhere too. That's for another non-alcoholic beer probably later. So I'm kind of a weird person. I like to make things as obnoxious as possible because I think it helps concepts stick in people's minds. I like alliterations mostly just because that's my own problem so deal with it. I want to talk today about placebo and what you probably think is a placebo is probably not because I don't know. A few weeks ago Alex called me up. He was like, I want you to give a talk on placebos. Like, okay, I'm a scientist and I'm a controlled trials and you know, I could look up placebo in the dictionary and I started reading more specific research regarding placebos. There's an entire field of study called placebo science. I don't know shit about placebo. So this is actually really fun for me because I have a terrible habit of accepting speaking engagements on topics I don't know anything about. And it's one of the things I do to kind of force myself into new fields and meet new people. It's super awesome for me and if I do say something wrong, call me out. I love it. I want to differentiate that placebos and the placebo effect are two different things. It's quite a contentious topic. There's like the equivalent of academic drive-by shootings going on and the battle rate is on for the fate of the universe and nobody really knows. It's really interesting. Academics get all lit. I'm not lit up about it right now because it's so crazy. It's quite an interesting amount of academic violence for something that is so fake. If it's fake, why do people care so much? I just want to like leave that out there for now and there's like, it's tumultuous, it's taboo, like, alright, this is cute. Why are people so upset about this and why do they have such strong opinions for something that doesn't exist? So speaking of fake, that's exactly what placebo's are, right? It's all fake. So I do a scientist and maybe a sugar pill used in the control group in the clinical trial, right? A requirement to prove that an intervention is actually worth a damn. It's something that you subtract from the baseline, get rid of all the artifactual stuff to see what the effect of the real intervention is, okay? To a physician, it may be the sugar pill to shut you up and get you out the door to get the next person in. In both of these cases, the placebo is viewed as a nuisance for the scientist. It's something to be subtracted to find the true effect for a physician. It might be something so you can subtract from your patient roster to help people who actually need it. But again, in both of these cases, it is considered a nuisance. And you know, something positive does happen and people do get better. So what the hell is going on? Press the wrong button. Well, when scientists measure the sugar pill group, the change is greater than zero, right? And that's something that I really want you to stick in your head because if it was fake, there would be no change. Something that a scientist is measuring is above zero. Like, I'm just going to repeat that over and over and over, all right? Something is happening. And sometimes it's so much greater than zero that it competes with the actual freaking drug, which is also something that's worth noting, okay? So when the patient with fake headaches or self-diagnosed boo boo belly take the subtherapeutic sham medication and they get measurable results, their pain does go away. Not like the pain in the sales sense, but like literal pain, like changes in their brain occur. Okay? So what gives? Does that mean that people have problems that are just all in their heads and that they just need a pat on the back? Is it because they want attention? You know, what is really happening if somebody really does feel pain from a variety of things and then that pain does go away? If it goes away from a placebo, does that mean their pain was fake? I don't know. Some people argue about either way, but like to me it's not. If I were to give an opinion, it's not fake, okay? So this is where things got interesting for me and I decided to dig and realize I have no idea what the hell's going on. So while the classic representation of placebo is that it's fake or inert or a more technical jargon possesses no therapeutic quality, bear in mind that there are measurable beneficial effects, then how can people get something measurably beneficial out of it? And that's because so many people treat the placebo as like a noun or an object, something tangible, right? This pill has nothing in it, therefore it does nothing, okay? But the placebo as a noun is different than the placebo effect, alright? And in more progressive circles, it's described as the meaning effect, alright? And from now on, I'm going to use the terms interchangeably in the context of the studies and stuff I'll be referencing. But if you start changing your frame of reference of placebo to meaning, then all of this stuff will take on a completely different tone. And I'm going to keep harping on that because now like academic people are starting to use this effect, more progressive academics, right? Not like the yogis or the Zen masters, but like real dorks. The white coat, where I'm going to read this out. The white coat wearing clipboard toting dokes with pocket protectors. Those are my people, right? So it's really not a matter of whether or not the placebo or meaning effect exists. But to me, it's how can we study it and then use it in a way that's beneficial for you guys or for physicians or whatever? How can you use this instead of make it a nuisance? If it gives people benefit, why would you try and erase it? I'm putting my CFO cap on, taking my scientist cap off, like why wouldn't you want to take advantage of something like that? So this is from Brody et al. Et al just means he's not the only person who wrote this, but he's the person who did the most work. This is one of the bioethicists that I dove into when he was talking about the placebo effect. And he says that the human brain seems to be hardwired to get better. And that certain sorts of mental stimuli capable of turning on this hardwired system. The elements that make up ritual seem to be especially effective in turning on these wiring circuits. And going back to what both Alex has talked about, the key word in here I want you to remember is ritual. This is how the meaning effect, rather than the placebo effect is described. So now we have the concept of meaning and we have the concept of ritual. These are two very important things and the tie-ins will come. But I'm sure you'll be able to fill in some blanks now. So I'm going to gum some stuff up with my own fancy sounding terminology that I'll flesh out. And it's that the meaning effect maintains transformative qualities in human physiology by allowing people to attach their experiences to symbols. When exposed to these symbols, taking a pill, physical interaction with somebody, the meaning elicits what's called the $10 word, the psychoneurobiological effect on the client. Okay, again, so what the hell does that really mean? All right? This means people are learning. This is what learning is. These associations, the imagery, the emotions, they are capable of activating your neurochemistry in a way that has an effect on your peripheral chemistry. Neurochemistry versus peripheral chemistry. Simply put, that means that the mind is interacting with the body. This is real stuff. The placebo effect or the meaning effect is built on trust. The trust in you, the trust you have in yourself, and the trust other people have in you. All right? So going back to learning, the quintessential example of associative learning is through Pavlov's beloved dogs. Hear to this? Feed the dog, ring the bell, drool, that stuff. That's good stuff. So ring the bell, feed the dog. Okay? Eventually the bell rings and the saliva comes aflown. The animals have associated the bell with food, and there is keyword a subconscious physiological response. Right? The dogs are not controlling the drool. The association with the bell triggers a physiological response. Right? Food causes drooling, right? Okay. So food causes drooling, but in this case it has nothing to do with food. It's because the bell is associated with the physiological response, even with food isn't involved. Okay? And although humans can be conditioned this way, pretty damn well actually, actually up until like age nine, you can treat them exactly like dogs. It's kind of like when you have a bedtime ritual and like you brush your teeth and then you have to take a poop, right? I told you there'd be poop stuff. So this is kind of a similar thing. Where you associate one action with a physiological response, it has absolutely nothing to do with the previous thing you did. What the hell does a toothbrush have to do with poop? Not much, right? But when there's a ritual involved, there is an association and there is meaning associated with that symbol and now you brush your teeth and got a poop. Like everybody has their own tooth brushing poop ritual, I'm sure, but this is the one that I think is pretty common, okay? Or pee here, whatever the case may be, okay? Shhh. Okay. So the meaning effect in humans when it comes to mind-body regulation is a bit more complicated and it has equally complicated words to match. That's okay. So I'm going to repeat what I said a couple of slides ago. It's that the meaning effect maintains transformative qualities in human physiology by allowing people to attach their experiences to symbols. When exposed to these symbols, the meaning elicits a psychoneurobiological effect on that. I'm sure, like, you drive by McDonald's and kids flip out, all right? That is the meaning effect in action. That symbol is associated with all sorts of whack-ass stuff going on in here for all sorts of people, good, positive, bad, whatever. That's just a classic example I use because I know, you know, people have been stuck in big, fat electromagnets and they show the McDonald's symbol and the Pleasure Center and the Brain lights up, all that good stuff. There is meaning in symbols, okay? Meaning in symbols and meaning in ritual. And you talk about all the retention stuff, you talk about all the training stuff, okay? You talk about the way you run your business, you know, the things that you say, all of these things end up having an impact. And I'll get a little bit more about that later. So to go back to the dog example, right, ring the bell, the dog drools, right? Again, the bell has nothing to do with food, but for whatever reason, the bell makes you drool. Cool. Well, with humans, you can think about the bell and start drooling. You do not have to actually hear it. That's pretty meta. Like, the power of the imagination draws on previous experiences, whether or not those experiences are real, okay? Have I had a nightmare freak you out? Same difference. You imagine something and have a response, okay? So even if the experience isn't real, you can draw from the imagination and nudge the body into responding in a way as if that event actually occurred, all right? Does that make sense? So I can start drooling by thinking about food rather than actually seeing it and smelling it. So it's, this is freaking cool. So you ever feel like throwing up from watching a gross YouTube video or, you know, something of that nature, right? Hearing your favorite song increase the quality of your workout? Does the anticipation of a gym launch call get your ass on Zoom? Does it ever rain right after you wash your car? Did hearing Alex talk make you tingle in the Netherlands regions? This is the meaning effect. Again, we're erasing placebo. It's a placebo meaning effect, right? You associate something with a symbol or some physical thing and then your body responds to it, okay? This is the meaning effect in action. It's not all sugar pills and aligning chakras, although they do have their places. That's for another conversation. That being said, something even cooler that I want to bring up, especially in terms of like what Layla spoke about too, is that in most interventions, we have a deep well of cultural influences we can draw upon, okay? And people do it subconsciously. No voodoo required, okay? No voodoo required. The cultural stuff is pretty neat. You'll get, this is cool. So for instance, blue is associated with being chilled, groovy energy, right? Blue is music. Or you're blue, you're sad, right? Red is associated with power and rage and fire and high energy output, like power ties, right? That's where this crap comes from. So it should be no surprise to you that red placebo pills perk people up. And blue placebo pills knock them out just because of the cultural association with the colors, okay? The meaning was already imbued into the intervention by cultural expectations. For Pete's sake, the PowerPoint symbol is red, okay? You're going to see it everywhere now. You're welcome. So in other words, to make this something kind of woo-woo, but it is what it is, the power of positive thinking is real, okay? Anchoring that feeling to real life experiences and objects amplifies that power. Does that make sense? Cool. So is the placebo effect all in your head? No. Well, yes. Crap. Well, I feel better who cares? That is the answer. Who gives a shit? It's good stuff. It's good. You can measure it. That's good enough for me. I like measuring stuff. So now that placebos, like the concept of the placebos and the meaning effective, imparts out a little bit, as in there's a physicality of it, and then the meaning effect, the placebo response, how can these two things be combined for maximum effectiveness? You have interventions, right? You have a system or a process or whatever. So that's kind of synonymous with intervention at this point. You're perturbing somebody's system. I'll call that an intervention. Meaning needs to be put in this intervention. Either you put meaning into it, or the client puts meaning into it, or the hairdresser puts meaning into it, or it's some combination of all those things. It always is, but you kind of want to fight for the stuff that's going to help you out more. So how the intervention is presented by the practitioner, using clinical terms, it's you. And what sort of intervention it is, like do people know what the hell you're trying to do before you do it? They might have preconceived associations with the sort of intervention you have, make a difference. So when the intervention is consumable, something like supplements, for example, drugs, supplements, things of that nature, a couple of questions you want to ask, the royal you, that I thought was interesting that I delve deeper into is that not only is there a placebo, the physical pill, I'll just use the pill because it's easiest to identify with, is that now there is a pure placebo and an impure placebo. And the concept of the impure placebo is something that is worth delving into a little bit more. So a pure placebo can be defined as your classic sugar pill. There's absolutely nothing in it that will have an effect on your physiology aside from the physicality of digesting whatever packing peanut you just ate. The impure placebo is a little bit different. This is where supplements can play a pivotal role in the transformative process of your members. An impure placebo is not necessarily inactive. In fact, it can be very active and contain ingredients that are proven to work. And this is where stuff gets pretty cool. The placebo effects from an impure placebo can be, I kind of have a loose definition of this, but basically it can be viewed as an amplified response to a lower dose of an active. So, for instance, if you need 50 milligrams of aspirin to work, maybe you only need 10 milligrams plus a placebo effect. Does that make sense? An amplified response of an already active substance is considered placebo. And the one that's cooler to me is that you can have a physiological effect that's completely unrelated to the active ingredients. And in terms of supplements, this is where the proverbial money is at, to me. It's that you can have a wide range of benefits from humble means. There's nothing deceptive about this at all. Just pure awesome from an impure placebo. So, this is what a science dork like me, for lack of a better known term to me, I call it pleiotropic. There's a genetic definition, all this other crap for it. But basically it's this one thing that's all sorts of weird crap. And you may not know what all those things are, but you know that this one thing does multiple things. And that's the word I use to describe impure placebos in the context of supplements, is that they can have a huge array of benefits that are completely outside of what maybe what they were designed to do. You want to use this. Specifically as I've heard Alex say on multiple occasions in regard to sales, that you want to use these powers for good. The convenient part about using these powers for good is that you're doing it already, without even knowing it. And ironically, not knowing it may make the effect even greater, but I'll get into that in a minute. I'll eat two packing peanuts and call me in the morning. So just remember everybody, hugs not drugs. Let's set the tone for the next section here. Hugs not drugs. So basically what this means is that your conviction, your relationship with your clients, how you interact with your clients, the atmosphere of your facility, the results that your clients share with each other. This is pivotal to the placebo effect or meaning response. This is how people imbue meaning into your interventions. Anything you do to increase the positive association with your brands, your methods, or your trainers, all of this matters. It all amplifies the results. So what's even more interesting is that the placebo effect is cumulative. Not like you just have one of these things that increases the placebo and then you don't have to do anything else. It's that the more placebo stuff you have, the better the effect. And this goes with imbuing your clients with positive expectations about the product and the consumption of your products. And I mean literal and figurative consumption, as in supplements and training and barbecues and all this other stuff I consider consuming your products. So to kind of prove my point, there's quite a few studies looking at this, clinical ones, is that there is a dose dependency of placebos, which is actually pretty funny. So four groups of people were given four different pills. Each group was told they have a varying concentration of stimulant, all the way from no stimulant to hella stimulant. None of the pills had anything in them. And guess what? There is quite literally a dose dependent response curve. The people who got zero felt nothing. The people who got two and a half felt a little bit. The people who got five felt a little bit more. And the people who got seven and a half felt jacked out of their minds. And these groups don't communicate with each other. So if they know that they're getting the highest dose, they are independently feeling the biggest effect with no interaction with any of the other groups. That is strange. And I'm not even going to pretend to figure out why I know this, like why, I don't know what's going on, but that doesn't matter. This is useful. So basically the more placebo people had, the more they were getting, the better it worked. And this leads me to my next point. What about hugs and drugs? Well, as it turns out, the effects are further amplified by positive reinforcement coming from the practitioner and the environment surrounding the intervention. So drugs work, hugs work, hugs plus drugs, that's the stuff. So in other words, the more involved a person is with your business, the more they drink your Kool-Aid, the more benefits your clients will receive. Not only because your methods are legitimate, but because the meaning you have put into these people's lives. You have put meaning into these people's lives. That's what that means. That's exactly what that means. So you can use the exact same methods on identical twins, genetically identical, and in a similar environment, and hugs with drugs will outperform drugs only every time. So the placebo effect is not separate from a physiological response, like the figment of your imagination crap. The placebo effect is a physiological response. It is no longer a binary. Well, this worked or no, it's placebo. But now this is effective and it's amplified with meaning. Now the more astute people are probably thinking this is sketchy in terms of practicing. So if you love me, you'll lie to me. And I'm sure a lot of you are probably thinking in those terms, but I want to address that and basically beat it to death. I've kind of pitted some examples against us already, but I'm sure most people are raising their eyebrows because of the preconceived notions they had about placebos and deception. You have to lie to a person, tell them it does this, and then if it does work, is that ethical or not? Yada, yada, yada. I just want to circumvent that entirely and give you some situations where this is not the case. And it's ironically enough, again, most effective for gym owners and impossible for physicians. So you're in a great position. So aren't placebos deceptive? Don't these scientists and doctors in clinical trials lie to people and then that's how these placebo responses are mediated? Yeah. Yeah. Is it ethical to lie to people even if it helps them? That's an interesting question and the drive-by shootings in academia rage on. One short answer to this question is, hell no, it is not okay. Short answer to this question is, hell yes, it is okay. It's tough to draw a line. So pretty helpful. I was super helpful, yes and no. Congratulations. So I want to pull back a minute and say, remember, this is not the placebo effect anymore. This is the meaning effect. And in randomized placebo controlled trials and other trickily conducted analytical psychology experiments and were considered clinical trials. The meaning is added through the deception, right? That's exactly correct. The doctors and the researchers lie to people and because of that lie, people got an effect. All right? But thankfully in the real world, especially in the world that you guys live in, it's not about deception at all. In your case, the meaning is added through fostering a positive environment where the trust in your authority and faith in your methods. Those are the things that give the meaning response. Going way back, it's built on trust, right? People trust doctors. So when the doctors lie to them, then the response is amplified, right? But they trust you. You don't have to lie to them and the response is amplified, okay? Because you're not giving people drugs. You're not giving people drugs. So doctors are intimidating in most circumstances. Their offices are even scarier. They're pretty sterile. There's no sense of community in the doctor's office. Even when they're packed, they're like, what is wrong with this person sitting next to me? You're sitting in a doctor's office filled with people. There's negative community in there, okay? The sterile environment is uncomfortable enough to make anybody right for the anxiety, right? There's nothing positive associated with being at the doctor's office. So what do doctors do? They do whatever the hell they can to get a positive response out of you, even if that means lying. And that is a totally different monster outright. All that stuff. I can talk about that later. But basically, I'm going to read this exactly, is that physicians and other practitioners treating conditions, they need to add meaning into a lot of the interventions they do through deception. The classic example might be warning people about side effects. You could get a sugar pill and say, watch out for dry mouth. But what happens when that person feels like their mouth is dry? That means that the treatment is working. So how whack ass is it that a person is looking out for negatives to see if their treatment's doing something positive? It blows my mind. But they're determining the efficacy of a treatment by how shitty they feel. You guys don't have to do that. You don't have to do that. You can actually feel your muscles growing. The meaning effect starts to work its magic. That's what that is. You've associated the meaning of this negative with a positive outcome, which is like, you really have to draw that out of people. It's imbued. People are looking for negatives and then somehow associating it with a positive. It's kind of disgusting. So the good news is that the meaning response in any properly run fitness facility is off of positive associations, not negative ones. Remember the doctor's office. The right fitness facility is the exact opposite of all that crap. And also, if you remember, you are not treating diseases. You are not prescribing and treating. The whole purpose is for you to increase the quality of a person's life. That's really it. And focusing on pharmacokinetics and clinical trials and biomechanics and all of this other stuff kind of undermines all of that. You just don't need to focus on that stuff to try and convince somebody the efficacy of an intervention. The doctor needs to scare people with big words, not you. You don't need to do that. If you need to use hard science to try and convince somebody the efficacy of your methods, you're going to go down a real dark path fast because somebody eventually is going to show up that knows more than you about it. And even if you're able to save yourself at a public setting, you got to go to sleep at night on that. I'll leave it there. Basically supplements and other fitness-related methods, they are not freaking drugs. Do not treat them that way, especially in regard to supplements. A lot of you see the marketing in regard to supplements. A lot of it is geared towards science and pharmacy and all of these signal transduction and all this other crap. Don't do that. They're not drugs. If you treat them like drugs, then it puts your clients in the mindset of drugs and pharmacy and medicine and doctors and all that other scary crap. Remember the phase you were at pleiotropic I used earlier? So that's exactly what's going on when you include all these positive things. So you can get a whole host of benefits unrelated to what the product is designed to do. And when you go into all of the science people think they know about a product, all you're doing is checking off or crossing out potential benefits this product may have. Does that make sense? Cool. Okay. Yeah. So embrace this pleiotropic effect coming from the meaning response. So this is exactly why supplements work so well. It's not because they're drugs. It's because they are not. And if that sticks, then your capacity to include supplements in all of your programs and the conviction associated with people using them, the results become astronomical. So again, it's no longer the binary of is it effective or is it a placebo. This is effective and it's amplified with meaning. So I hope by now you can kind of maybe see some benefits of trying to implement things like supplements into your programs. This isn't about randomized clinical trials or bro scientists and signal transduction and keto and all this other crap people think they know things about. It is about professionals with a pension for performance. That's what you guys are. And I could argue it's a moral imperative to do every flipping thing you can to try and get performance out of the people you work with. And you do that by giving the things meaning. So take a step back. Let's think about how you imbue meaning or how you can imbue meaning. Chances are you guys are doing all these things already or some of them. But if you look at it from another perspective, it may give you a little more hood spa. This is Adele. Okay. Adele is a pretty good singer. It's kind of famous. All right. So would you tell Adele that she's doing 150 repetitions over 18 sets in 60 minutes? Or do you tell her she has an hour with a qualified professional that's assigned a program specifically to meet her goals? Plus Siba. Just, you know. That's not deception. That's imbuing something positive in one of your methods, right? And eliminating the technical crap that you think people think is important. All right. So it's not hard to translate a lot of the things you do to something a little bit more positive that can resonate with people. All right. This includes things like supplements. But again, if you think about the sets and reps and minutes and the buttons and the shiny stuff, how can you pull away from the technical jargon and replace it with something equivalent that's more positive rather than neutral? Or trying to put yourself in a position above these people? Because you're already in a position of authority. You don't need to pound that into that. So I'll paraphrase Alex here when I say the vacation is more important than the ride on the plane. All the technical crap that people talk about, that's standing in line. That's checking your bag. That's not having enough leg room. But an hour with a qualified professional that gives a damn about you, that's pretty cool. I could look forward to something like that. So educating a person on a product will help them is different than giving people technical specifications. And I really want to pound that in. How something helps you versus what a product is supposed to do are not exactly the same thing. And educating a person on how a product will help them is what gives people expectations. And these expectations are what initiate the meaning response. So you as the authority figure, you bring this positive environment about, you foster it. You determine whether something is a pocket full of sunshine or a bucket of bullshit. You have that power. I mean, really? Yeah. So remember how people determine the treatment's efficacy by the negatives. But the side effects are given and things like that. Just turn the frown upside down. I think that's probably a good way to make it concise. If people are looking for something, they will find it. That's all that means. And if you give people positive expectations, they will live into that. That is the meaning response. That is the placebo effect. This has been proven multiple times over in regard to deception. So I want to make this clear that there are now things called open label trials, which sounds exactly what it sounds like. This is where people are told upfront that they're not getting a drug specifically designed for the condition they have. And this clinical trial was IBS. I told you there'd be more poop stuff. So they were given something that was not specifically meant for IBS at all. The researchers were very specific about saying this is not designed for IBS, but you'll get some benefit out of it because there's meaning associated with the placebo response. And the next group, they were given a placebo and said there's nothing in it. They probably won't do anything, whatever. And then a third group, they actually got a placebo. They got a deceptive placebo. They were actually saying this is drug X that makes your IBS better. And what I want to point out is that the open label trial where the groups knew they were getting a supplement that was not specifically designed for IBS, but may show benefit, they got damn close results to the deception group. Because the person that administered the treatment had a position of authority and they trusted them and that they knew what they were taking was not specifically meant for IBS, but because there was certain stuff in it that had positive effects on other physiological systems in their body that it may actually end up helping them. And this is where pleotropy becomes important. Just because it's not specifically designed for something that's wrong with you doesn't mean it won't have a positive effect. They didn't have to do it with immunomodulators or steroids, even the fun kind, or antidepressants or anything like that. This is just the classic sugar, not the classic sugar pill. It was a supplement. But that's just something I really want to pound in, is that the group that was lied to didn't have a hell of a lot of difference from the group that wasn't lied to. It's because the researchers imbued the supplements with meaning. And you imbue your own products with meaning, but I'm willing to bet you had no idea how strong of an impact it had. This whole psycho-neurobiology thing is a frickin' trip. So pay closer attention to the things you say and how you imbue meaning into your products. And again, products aren't necessarily things you eat. But everything you do, say, and think about the products you have, that bleeds over to the people you serve. And the more positive it is, the more positive association they're going to have and the bigger of a meaning response they will get. Does that make sense? Cool. So to kind of parse this down a bit, by you sharing the experiences you have had with a supplement, for example, like a positive experience you have had with something does give people positive expectations, okay? Supplement training program, whatever. And what other people have thought about your products, too. Similar thing. So you provide education that leads to expectations, and these expectations are what initiate the meaning effect, or the meaning response. Placebo response, placebo effect, whatever the hell it is now, okay? So there are short-term and long-term plays in regard to placebo. And I want to cover those pretty fast. And I believe that there's different ways to approach this, okay? So in the short term, the very short term, I think the meaning effect and its efficacy is best for an immediate physiological response, okay? One, that is damn cool, okay? And two, like this is probably the quintessential mind-body regulation problem. It's not unlike getting a slap in the back of the head before you do a heavy deadlift to get aroused. I'm going to use the term aroused in a technical sense, obviously. Two, this is the strongest tool you have as a coach to break down mental barriers people have as far as what they are capable of, okay? If people have negative expectations, their brain will limit their performance. That is quite literally what that means. And part of it's because it keeps you from injury, among other things. But basically, the whisper you have in a person's ear can change this person's neurochemistry to the point where their nervous system operates differently. That's cool. Like, and it's seen in stuff you already know, right? It's cheering somebody on, it's slapping them on the back, yada, yada, yada. But I hope you kind of have a little more appreciative of what that really means, okay? So in three groups of elite cyclists, one group was given a carbohydrate supplement and told it was just artificially sweetened water, all right? So these people were given carbs. People know carbs are good for performance, right? But they were given carbohydrates and told it was sweet water with nothing in it, okay? The next group, they were given a carbohydrate supplement and told it was a carbohydrate supplement. The third group, they were given a carbohydrate supplement and told them it could be either, we don't know. And then they put them on the bicycles to death, all right? Oh, the group in the either category, the either group three could be either, there is no change in performance. And carbohydrates are a scientifically validated, ergogenic aid. I consume carbohydrates during endurance, my power input goes up, this has been proven a hundred, gazillion million times. If the researcher says, maybe there's supplements in here, it doesn't do anything. So what this tells us is that uncertain information weakens positive expectations people have. That's pretty crazy, okay? What are the implications of this? Really? I'll get to in a second. The group that was given a carbohydrate supplement and told it was a carbohydrate supplement, their performance went up. And given that the group that were given a carbohydrate supplement and nothing changed, what do you think that difference is? Ryan, what do you think the difference is? Yeah. It's because they were given meaning. They were given meaning, okay? And the real kicker to me is that the group that was given carbs, which is a scientifically validated intervention in terms of increasing performance, when they were told it was a placebo, they performed worse than baseline. That is insanity. Does this mean carbs don't work? Absolutely not. It means that the negative expectations outweigh the efficacy of a scientifically proven intervention. Okay? It doesn't mean carbohydrate supplements don't work. It means that the person's mind-body connection can override those seated with the meaning effect. It's because, quite literally, the power of positive thinking. That's literally what it is. You want to boil it down. And I hope you guys know this, like, Bill and Ted. I just, like, tried to figure out a way to squeeze this in. Is that when there's enough meaning associated with your intervention, the placebo response will act as a psychological time machine. And I couldn't think of a better picture to integrate. Because they literally went back in time, got Sigmund Freud, and brought him back. Okay? Basically, if you're keeping a person comfortable while they're making progress, then they're not going to be as obsessed with the progress that they make. And in the long term, the progress they make will be greater. And the more they like you, yada, yada, yada. So growth, all that cool stuff. Okay? Well, basically, the practical implications of this, like longer lifetime value, greater adherence, you know, things like that. But basically, the person ends up loving fitness because of the meaning that you have given them. Okay? And they don't hate it and use it as a means to an end to try and get it in a new dress. That may have hooked the person, but it's probably not what's keeping them. Okay? So when the placebo effect or the meaning response is properly integrated into your programming through all the avenues you have, the palliative aspect of the meaning response will have people happy. Happy is good. What did the deans say? Don't be a dick. Solid advice. Okay? And you solve their pain, right? It's not because they were fat.
